challenges.hint: Check divisibility by 15 first, then 3, then 5.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need a function that classifies numbers by divisibility rules.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Check divisibility by 15 first (both 3 and 5), then by 3, then by 5, and default to the number as a string.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: Running with n=15 prints FizzBuzz since 15 is divisible by both 3 and 5.

challenges.title: Dupe Purge
challenges.description: Write function removeDuplicates(arr) that removes duplicate values from an array. Test with [1,2,2,3,3,3]. Expected output: `1,2,3`.
challenges.hint: A Set automatically removes duplicates.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to remove duplicate values while preserving order.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Convert the array to a Set (which only keeps unique values), then spread it back into an array.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: Running the code prints 1,2,3 with duplicates removed.

challenges.title: Factorial
challenges.description: Write function factorial(n) that returns n factorial. Test with factorial(5). Expected output: `120`.
challenges.hint: Multiply all integers from 1 to n in a loop.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to compute the product of all positive integers up to n.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Initialize a result variable to 1 and multiply by each integer from 2 to n.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: Running with n=5 gives 1*2*3*4*5 = 120, matching the expected output.

challenges.title: Digit Sum
challenges.description: Write function digitSum(n) that returns the sum of all digits. Test with digitSum(1234). Expected output: `10`.
challenges.hint: Convert to string, split into characters, then sum the numeric values.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to sum the individual digits of a number.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Convert the number to a string, split into characters, convert each back to a number, and sum with reduce.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: Running with 1234 gives 1+2+3+4 = 10, matching the expected output.

challenges.title: Palindrome
challenges.description: Write function isPalindrome(str) that checks if a string reads the same forwards and backwards. Test with "racecar". Expected output: `true`.
challenges.hint: Reverse the string and compare to the original.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to check if a string is the same when reversed.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Split the string into characters, reverse the array, join back, and compare to the original.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: Running with "racecar" prints true since it reads the same backwards.

challenges.title: Word Count
challenges.description: Write function countWords(str) that returns the number of words in a string. Test with "hello world foo". Expected output: `3`.
challenges.hint: Split on whitespace and count the resulting pieces.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to count the number of words separated by whitespace.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Trim the string and split on one or more whitespace characters using a regex, then return the length.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: Running with the test string prints 3, matching the expected output.

challenges.title: Array Sum
challenges.description: Write function sum(arr) using reduce to sum all elements. Test with [1,2,3,4,5]. Expected output: `15`.
challenges.hint: Use Array.prototype.reduce with an accumulator starting at 0.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to compute the total of all array elements.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Use reduce with a callback that adds each value to an accumulator starting at 0.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: Running with [1,2,3,4,5] gives 1+2+3+4+5 = 15.

challenges.title: Fib Number
challenges.description: Write function fibonacci(n) that returns the nth Fibonacci number (0-indexed: fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1). Test with fibonacci(7). Expected output: `13`.
challenges.hint: Use two variables and iterate, updating them each step.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to compute the nth Fibonacci number iteratively.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Start with a=0 and b=1, then loop from 2 to n, shifting values forward each iteration.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: The sequence is 0,1,1,2,3,5,8,13 so fib(7)=13, matching the expected output.

challenges.title: Sort Squad
challenges.description: Write function sortDesc(arr) that returns a new array sorted in descending order. Test with [3,1,4,1,5]. Expected output: `5,4,3,1,1`.
challenges.hint: Use sort with a comparator (b - a) for descending order.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to sort numbers from largest to smallest without mutating the original array.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Spread into a new array and sort with comparator (a, b) => b - a for descending order.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: Running the code prints 5,4,3,1,1, confirming descending order.

challenges.title: Truncator
challenges.description: Write function truncate(str, maxLen) that keeps the first maxLen characters and adds "..." if the string is longer. Test with truncate("JavaScript is fun", 10). Expected output: `JavaScript...`.
challenges.hint: Use slice to keep the first maxLen characters, then append "...".

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to shorten a string if it exceeds a maximum length.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Check if the string is longer than maxLen. If so, slice it and append "...".

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: Running the code prints JavaScript... since "JavaScript" is 10 characters plus "...".

challenges.title: Char Count

challenges.hint: Split into characters and filter for matches.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to count how many times a specific character appears in a string.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Split the string into an array of characters and filter for those matching the target character.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: Running with "banana" and "a" prints 3, since "a" appears three times.

challenges.title: Common Ground

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: Running the code prints 3,4, which are the common elements.

challenges.description: Write function createCounter(start) that returns an object with increment(), decrement(), and getCount() methods using closures. Test: create counter at 0, increment 3 times, decrement once, print getCount(). Expected output: `2`.

challenges.title: Power Up
challenges.description: Write a recursive function power(base, exp) that computes base raised to exp. Test with power(2, 10). Expected output: `1024`.
challenges.hint: Base case: exp === 0 returns 1. Recursive case: base * power(base, exp - 1).

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to compute exponentiation using recursion.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: When exp is 0, return 1. Otherwise return base * power(base, exp - 1).

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: Running with (2, 10) gives 2^10 = 1024.

challenges.title: Deep Clone
challenges.description: Write function deepClone(obj) using recursion to create a deep copy. Test by cloning {a: 1, b: {c: 2}}, modifying the clone, and printing the original b.c. Expected output: `2`.
challenges.hint: Recursively clone each property. Handle null, non-objects, arrays, and plain objects.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need a recursive deep clone that creates fully independent copies of nested objects.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Check for null and non-objects as base cases, create an array or object, and recursively clone each property.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: After modifying the clone, original.b.c is still 2, proving the deep clone works.

challenges.title: Curry Up
challenges.description: Write function curry(fn) that curries a function. Test with a curried add(a, b, c) = a + b + c by calling curry(add)(1)(2)(3). Expected output: `6`.
challenges.hint: Return a curried function that collects arguments until fn.length is reached.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to transform a multi-argument function into a chain of single-argument calls.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Return a function that accumulates arguments. When enough are collected (>= fn.length), call the original function.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: Calling curry(add)(1)(2)(3) accumulates [1], [1,2], [1,2,3] then calls add(1,2,3) = 6.

challenges.title: Memo Cache
challenges.description: Write function memoize(fn) that caches results based on arguments. Test with a memoized factorial function, calling factorial(5). Expected output: `120`.
challenges.hint: Use a Map to store results keyed by JSON.stringify of the arguments.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to cache function results to avoid redundant computation.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Create a Map-based cache. Serialize arguments as a key, check cache before computing, and store results.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: factorial(5) computes 5! = 120, and subsequent calls with same args return cached results.

challenges.title: Zip Lock

challenges.title: Trie Build
challenges.description: Implement a Trie class with insert(word) and search(word) methods. Test inserting "cat", "car", "card" and searching for "car" and "cab". Expected output: `true` followed by `false`.
challenges.hint: Each node is an object mapping characters to child nodes, with an isEnd flag.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need a prefix tree that supports efficient word insertion and exact word lookup.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Each node is an object of character-to-child mappings. Insert traverses/creates nodes; search traverses and checks isEnd.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: "car" was inserted so search returns true; "cab" was not, so it returns false.

challenges.title: Graph Walk
challenges.description: Write function bfs(graph, start) that returns nodes in BFS traversal order. Test with {A:["B","C"], B:["D"], C:["E"], D:[], E:[]} starting from "A". Expected output: `A,B,C,D,E`.
challenges.hint: Use a queue and a visited set. Process nodes level by level.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to traverse a graph breadth-first, visiting all nodes reachable from the start.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Use a queue initialized with start. Dequeue a node, add to results, and enqueue unvisited neighbors.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: BFS visits A first, then its neighbors B and C, then D and E, giving A,B,C,D,E.

challenges.title: Rate Gate
challenges.description: Write function createRateLimiter(maxCalls, windowMs) that returns a function allowing at most maxCalls within a sliding time window. Call it 3 times synchronously with a limit of 2 — the third call exceeds the limit. Expected output: `true`, `true`, then `false`.
challenges.hint: Track call timestamps and remove expired ones before checking the limit.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need a function that enforces a maximum call rate within a sliding time window.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Store timestamps of each call. Remove expired timestamps, then check if the count is below maxCalls.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: First two calls succeed (true, true), third exceeds the limit (false).

challenges.title: Linked Cycle
challenges.description: Write function hasCycle(head) that detects a cycle in a linked list using Floyd's tortoise and hare algorithm. Test with a cyclic list. Expected output: `true`.
challenges.hint: Use two pointers: slow moves one step, fast moves two steps. If they meet, there is a cycle.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to detect a cycle in a linked list without using extra space.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Use Floyd's algorithm: move slow pointer one step and fast pointer two steps. If they meet, a cycle exists.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: The list has node3 pointing back to node1, creating a cycle, so the output is true.

challenges.title: Tree Balance
challenges.description: Write function isBalanced(root) that checks if a binary tree is height-balanced (no subtree differs in height by more than 1). Expected output: `true`.
challenges.hint: Write a helper that returns height or -1 if unbalanced. Check from the bottom up.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to check whether a binary tree is height-balanced using recursive height computation.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Compute height bottom-up. Return -1 if any subtree is unbalanced; otherwise return max child height + 1.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: The test tree has max height difference of 1, so it is balanced and prints true.

challenges.title: JSON Path
challenges.description: Write function jsonPath(obj, path) that retrieves a value from a nested object using a dot-notation path string. Test with {a:{b:{c:42}}} and path "a.b.c". Expected output: `42`.
challenges.hint: Split the path by dots and reduce through the object, accessing each key in turn.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to navigate a nested object using a string path like 'a.b.c'.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Split the path on dots and use reduce to traverse each key in sequence.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: Traversing a -> b -> c yields 42, matching the expected output.

challenges.title: Retry Logic
challenges.description: Write function retry(fn, retries) that calls fn up to retries times, returning the first successful result. Test with a function that succeeds on the 3rd attempt. Expected output: `Success on attempt 3`.
challenges.hint: Loop up to retries times, catch exceptions, and rethrow if all attempts fail.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need a retry mechanism that catches errors and retries until success or exhaustion.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Loop from 1 to retries. Try calling fn, catch errors, and rethrow on the last attempt.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: The function fails twice and succeeds on attempt 3, printing Success on attempt 3.

challenges.title: Custom Map
challenges.description: Implement myMap(arr, fn) from scratch without using Array.prototype.map. Test by doubling [1,2,3]. Expected output: `2,4,6`.
challenges.hint: Loop through the array and apply fn to each element, pushing results into a new array.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to reimplement the map function using a basic for loop.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Iterate through the array, call fn with (element, index, array), and collect results.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: Doubling [1,2,3] gives [2,4,6], which joins to 2,4,6.

challenges.title: Custom Reduce
challenges.description: Implement myReduce(arr, fn, initial) from scratch without using Array.prototype.reduce. Test by summing [1,2,3,4]. Expected output: `10`.
challenges.hint: Start with initial value (or first element if undefined) and iterate through the array.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to reimplement reduce from scratch, handling both with and without an initial value.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: If initial is undefined, use the first element. Then iterate from the appropriate start index, accumulating results.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: Summing [1,2,3,4] with initial 0 gives 0+1+2+3+4 = 10.

challenges.title: Iterator Make
challenges.description: Create function fibIterator(limit) that returns an iterable yielding Fibonacci numbers up to limit. Test with limit 10. Expected output: `1,1,2,3,5,8`.
challenges.hint: Return an object with [Symbol.iterator]() that returns a next() method following the iterator protocol.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need a custom iterable that produces Fibonacci numbers up to a given limit.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Implement the iterable protocol with [Symbol.iterator]() returning an object with next(). Track two variables for the Fibonacci sequence.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: Fibonacci numbers up to 10 are 1,1,2,3,5,8, matching the expected output.

challenges.title: DFS Path
challenges.description: Write function dfsPath(graph, start, end) that finds a path between two nodes using depth-first search. Test with {A:["B","C"], B:["D"], C:[], D:["E"], E:[]} from "A" to "E". Expected output: `A,B,D,E`.
challenges.hint: Use recursive DFS with a visited set. Build the path as you go and return it when the end node is found.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to find a path in a graph from start to end using depth-first search.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Recursively visit neighbors, building the path. When the end node is reached, return the path. Backtrack on dead ends.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: DFS finds the path A -> B -> D -> E, printing A,B,D,E.
